content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES ConwissIoN
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
Fax (916) 322-0886
May 27, 1998
Thomas J. McGlynn
Law Offices of McGlynn and McGlynn
737 Washington Street
Post Office Box 1110
Red Bluff, California 96080
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-98-119
Dear Mr. McGlynn:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Fair Board member Larry
Smith regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1 As I informed you on
May 4, 1998, Government Code Section 1090 is not within the jurisdiction of the Commission.
The Attorney General is charged with the interpretation and enforcement ofthat code section.
Also, please note that the Commission does not provide advice regarding conduct that has
already occurred. (Regulation 18329.) Finally, the Commission does not act as the finder of fact
in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
QUESTION
May Fair Board member Larry Smith purchase 10.87 acres from the county immediately
adjacent to property leased from the county by the fair board?
CONCLUSION
Yes. Nothing in the Act prohibits Mr. Smith from purchasing the property. Of course,
once he purchases the property Mr. Smith may not participate in a governmental decision that
will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on his economic interest, the
10.87 acres.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-98-119
Page 2
FACTS
Larry Smith is a member of the Fair Board (“board”) for the 30th Agricultural District in
Tehama County. Mr. Smith was appointed by the governor and has served on the board for some
period of time. The board oversees the conducting of the Tehama County Fair (“fair”) upon the
fairgrounds owned by the County of Tehama (“county”), but leased to the board.
The lease between the county and the board calls for a rental of $10 per year for 25 years.
The lease specifically provides that the county shall have the right to use the leased premises
when such use will not in any manner, directly or indirectly, interfere with the use of the
premises by the board. You state that the intent of this provision, as expressed in the lease, is to
prohibit any endeavors competing with the fair from being conducted on the fairgrounds.
The county owns 10.87 acres located adjacent to the fairgrounds. Mr. Smith has put
together a private investment group to purchase the 10.87 acres. His group’s plan for
development is to establish a recreational vehicle park, catering primarily to horse owners, on the
site. Due to the site being adjacent to the fairgrounds, there would be a mutual benefit between
the fairgrounds and the RV park. For instance, any person using the proposed RV park, who had
a horse that needed to be boarded, would have access to the fairgrounds to board the horse. In
effect, if the development of the RV park takes place, it will produce income to the board.
Mr. Smith and his investment group have now contracted to buy the property, but are not
completing the purchase until Mr. Smith can make absolutely certain that he is not in a conflict
of interest situation.
The development of the site, as presently designed, would involve the county giving an
easement over a portion ofthe county fairgrounds. This would be over an existing road on the
fairgrounds, and would be given in exchange for an easement over the site property, which would
benefit not only the county, but also the fair. The board’s involvement in the granting of the
easement would, at best, be simply approving the granting of the easement by the county, and/or
indicating that the granting of the easement does not interfere with the board’s lease. Mr. Smith
would not participate in any action by the board, either as to the easement or the site property
(although, as stated, no action by the board as to the site property is contemplated).
There is the possibility that in the development of the site property the board would also
receive a benefit as to a percentage of the rental income from the property. This has been
discussed in general, but no decision has yet been made. This would bestow an additional
benefit on the board, but without any actual commitment of any kind on the part of the board.
There is also a possibility that a percentage of income from the developed site may also be
required to go to the county, either in total or in part.


File No. A-98-119
Page 3
ANALYSIS
The Act was adopted by the people of the State of California by initiative in 1974. The
purpose of the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure that public officials perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests ofpersons who support them. (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of this goal,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in malcing, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
fmancial interest. Nothing in the Act prohibits him from purchasing the land in question from
the county.
2
You have stated that Mr. Smith will not participate in any decision regarding the 10.87
acres. Regulations 18700 and 18700.1 define when a public official makes a governmental
decision, participates in a governmental decision, or uses his or her official position to influence
a governmental decision.
I have enclosed those definitions for your infonnation. Also, I have
enclosed a guide to the Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions for your reference.
Please note that if Mr. Smith does invest in the 10.87 acres and/or runs a business from
the property, many decisions that he will make as a fair board member may have a reasonably
foreseeable and material financial effect on his property and/or his business because of the close
proximity of the 10.87 acres to the location of the fair and because the services that he would
provide are directly linked to the fair. (See, for example, Regulation 18702.3.) This would lead
to his disqualification from many of the fair board’s decisions.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Marte Castafios
SOC :MC:tls
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
2 Government Code Section 1090, on the other hand, may prohibit Mr. Smith outright from purchasing the
land from the county or contracting with the board. Again, please contact the Attorney General regarding
Government Code Section 1090.
I:Advice Ltrs\981 19.wpd
